title: Honey-roast carrots and parsnips
subtitle: Christmas-trimmings standard, sweet and caramelised
excerpt: Carrots and parsnips par-cooked then roasted with honey and butter until their edges caramelise and the honey turns to a sticky glaze. The most reliable vegetable side for the Christmas table.
sourceNotes: Classic British roast-vegetable preparation. Honey-glazed root vegetables are well documented in twentieth-century British home-cooking tradition and in Margaret Costa's Four Seasons Cookery Book (1970), a key reference for the British Christmas table.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Par-boil the vegetables up to 24 hours ahead and keep in the fridge. Toss in the honey-butter mixture and roast when needed.

glossaryTerms.definition: To partially cook in boiling water, stopping before the food is fully cooked. Used before roasting to ensure even cooking and a tender interior.

content.text: The par-boil step is what separates honey-roast root vegetables that caramelise properly from those that spend 45 minutes in the oven still slightly undercooked in the middle. A 5-minute blanch before roasting means the inside is already tender when the outside starts to colour; the honey glaze then has time to become sticky and concentrated without the vegetables drying out.

Parsnips should be halved lengthways and quartered for larger ones; carrots cut on the diagonal into even pieces about 6 cm long. Even sizing is the main source of uneven cooking. The honey goes on towards the end of the roast, not at the beginning: added too early, it burns before the vegetables are cooked through.

items.prepNote: peeled, cut on the diagonal into 5 cm pieces
peeled, halved lengthways (quartered if large), woody cores removed

content.text: Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Bring a large pan of salted water to the boil. Add {{carrot}} prepared carrots and {{parsnip}} prepared parsnips and
for 5 minutes. Drain well and leave to steam-dry in the colander for 2 minutes. Steam-drying prevents the oil from spitting in the oven and helps the surfaces brown.

Toss the drained vegetables in {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large roasting tin. Season generously and spread in a single layer. Roast for 20 minutes, turning once halfway, until beginning to colour at the edges.

Mix {{honey}} of honey with {{unsalted-butter}} of melted butter. Pour over the vegetables and turn to coat. Tuck in the thyme sprigs if using. Return to the oven for a further 15 to 20 minutes until the vegetables are golden and the glaze is sticky and caramelised. Watch the oven in the last 5 minutes; honey burns quickly once it has reduced.

items.cause: Added too early in the roast, or oven too hot in the final stage
items.fix: Add the honey glaze only in the last 15 to 20 minutes of roasting. If the tin shows signs of burning, add a splash of water and scrape the base.

items.cause: Pieces are different sizes, or the tin is overcrowded
items.fix: Use a larger tin or two tins — overcrowded vegetables steam rather than roast. Cut vegetables to uniform size.

items.symptom: Parsnips are soft but not golden
items.cause: Pan is too crowded, or oven temperature is too low
items.fix: Transfer to a hotter area of the oven or increase the temperature to 220°C for the final 10 minutes.

items.symptom: Carrot centres still hard after full cooking time
items.cause: Carrots were cut too thickly or par-boiling step was skipped
items.fix: Return to the oven for another 10 minutes. Next time, cut to 5 cm maximum and par-boil.

content.text: Honey-roast root vegetables moved into the mainstream British Christmas table during the 1970s and 1980s, as honey became a standard storecupboard item and the repertoire of Sunday-roast accompaniments expanded. Carrots and parsnips are the classic pairing: the parsnip's natural sweetness amplifies the honey, and the carrot provides colour contrast. They go alongside goose and duck as naturally as they do beside the Christmas turkey, and they are one of the few Christmas-dinner components that do not require last-minute attention.
